title: Record sales

infobox.value: or **music sales** are activities related to selling music recordings ([albums](/wiki/Album "Album"), [singles](/wiki/Single_(music) "Single (music)"), or [music videos](/wiki/Music_video#Commercial_release "Music video")) through physical [record shops](/wiki/Record_shop "Record shop") or [digital music stores](/wiki/Digital_music_store "Digital music store").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Record sales reached their peak in 1999, when 600 million people spent an average of $64 on records, achieving $40 billion in sales of recorded music.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Economic activity related to selling music recordings

"Album sales" redirects here. For the Billboard chart, see [Top Album Sales](/wiki/Top_Album_Sales "Top Album Sales").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Tower_Records_Nagoya_Kintetsu_Pass%27e_ac_%282%29.jpg/330px-Tower_Records_Nagoya_Kintetsu_Pass%27e_ac_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tower_Records_Nagoya_Kintetsu_Pass%27e_ac_(2).jpg)

A [Tower Records](/wiki/Tower_Records "Tower Records") store in [Nagoya](/wiki/Nagoya "Nagoya"), Japan, November 2023.

**Record sales** or **music sales** are activities related to selling music recordings ([albums](/wiki/Album "Album"), [singles](/wiki/Single_(music) "Single (music)"), or [music videos](/wiki/Music_video#Commercial_release "Music video")) through physical [record shops](/wiki/Record_shop "Record shop") or [digital music stores](/wiki/Digital_music_store "Digital music store").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Record sales reached their peak in 1999, when 600 million people spent an average of $64 on records, achieving $40 billion in sales of recorded music.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Record sales started declining in the 21st century,[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) which made artists rely on [touring](/wiki/Concert_tour "Concert tour") for most of their income.[[8]](#cite_note-8) By 2019, record sales accounted for less than half of global recorded music revenue, overtaken by [streaming](/wiki/Music_streaming "Music streaming").[[9]](#cite_note-9) Following the inclusion of streaming into record charts in the mid-2010s, record sales are also referred to as **traditional sales** or **pure sales**.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Although an accurate worldwide sales figure is hard to determine, it is widely acknowledged that [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") have sold more records than [any other artist](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_music_artists "List of best-selling music artists") in history.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson")'s studio album *[Thriller](/wiki/Thriller_(Michael_Jackson_album) "Thriller (Michael Jackson album)")* (1982) remains the [best-selling album](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_albums "List of best-selling albums") in history,[[17]](#cite_note-17) while "[White Christmas](/wiki/White_Christmas_(song) "White Christmas (song)")" (1942) performed by [Bing Crosby](/wiki/Bing_Crosby "Bing Crosby") is believed to be the [best-selling single](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_singles "List of best-selling singles").[[18]](#cite_note-Guinness-18)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Record_sales&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1870s–2000s: Physical era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Record_sales&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1870s–2000s: Physical era")]

See also: [History of sound recording](/wiki/History_of_sound_recording "History of sound recording") and [Album era](/wiki/Album_era "Album era")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/1943_-_Speedys_Record_Shop_Ad_-_5_Feb_MC_-_Allentown_PA.jpg/250px-1943_-_Speedys_Record_Shop_Ad_-_5_Feb_MC_-_Allentown_PA.jpg)](/wiki/File:1943_-_Speedys_Record_Shop_Ad_-_5_Feb_MC_-_Allentown_PA.jpg)

A [record shop](/wiki/Record_shop "Record shop") advertisement from 1943

Before the existence of [recording medium](/wiki/Recording_medium "Recording medium") and its player, the music industry earned profit through selling [musical compositions](/wiki/Musical_composition "Musical composition") on [sheet music](/wiki/Sheet_music "Sheet music"). The very first sales chart published by *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* magazine in the United States was the *Sheet Music Best Sellers* chart.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Following the invention of the [phonograph](/wiki/Phonograph "Phonograph"), by [Thomas Edison](/wiki/Thomas_Edison "Thomas Edison") in 1877,[[20]](#cite_note-20) the music industry began hiring [singers](/wiki/Singers "Singers") to record songs made by composers. Due to the length limit of recording media, [singles](/wiki/Single_(music) "Single (music)") were the only available commercial releases. In 1900, the US record sales is estimated at 3 million copies. The music industry continued its growth, and by 1921 the value of record sales in the US reached $106 million with 140 million records being sold.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Album sales were first reported by *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* magazine on March 24, 1945.[[22]](#cite_note-billboard200-22) An album was then defined as a box containing a set of singles, such as *[Glenn Miller](/wiki/Glenn_Miller_(1945_album) "Glenn Miller (1945 album)")* by [Glenn Miller](/wiki/Glenn_Miller "Glenn Miller"), as well as *[Selections from Going My Way](/wiki/Selections_from_Going_My_Way "Selections from Going My Way")* and *[Merry Christmas](/wiki/Merry_Christmas_(Bing_Crosby_album) "Merry Christmas (Bing Crosby album)")* by [Bing Crosby](/wiki/Bing_Crosby "Bing Crosby"). Technological developments in the early 20th century led to the development of the vinyl [LP record](/wiki/LP_record "LP record") as an important medium for recorded music. In 1948, [Columbia Records](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records") began to bring out 331⁄3 rpm twelve-inch extended-play LPs that could play for as long as 52 minutes, or 26 minutes per side. [Musical film](/wiki/Musical_film "Musical film") soundtracks, jazz works, and thematic albums by singers such as [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra") quickly utilized the new longer format. *Billboard* launched its first regularly published weekly albums chart, [Best Selling Popular Albums](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200"), on March 24, 1956.[[22]](#cite_note-billboard200-22)

During the 1950s and into the 1960s, 45 rpm [seven-inch single](/wiki/Seven-inch_single "Seven-inch single") sales were considered the primary market for the recording industry, while albums were a secondary market. The careers of notable rock and roll performers such as [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley") were driven primarily by single sales. Album sales reached its peak in English-language popular music from the mid-1960s to the mid-2000s (known as "[album era](/wiki/Album_era "Album era")") in which the album was the dominant form of recorded music expression and consumption.[[23]](#cite_note-23) From the early 1980s, the [CD](/wiki/CD "CD") became the dominant format for both albums and singles, due to factors such as higher audio quality, durability, ease of use, and longer playing time. In 1986, [Dire Straits](/wiki/Dire_Straits "Dire Straits")' *[Brothers in Arms](/wiki/Brothers_in_Arms_(album) "Brothers in Arms (album)")* became the first album in history to sell over one million copies on CD.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### 2000s–present: Digital era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Record_sales&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2000s–present: Digital era")]

See also: [Music download](/wiki/Music_download "Music download") and [Vinyl revival](/wiki/Vinyl_revival "Vinyl revival")

In October 2005, "[Hollaback Girl](/wiki/Hollaback_Girl "Hollaback Girl")" by [Gwen Stefani](/wiki/Gwen_Stefani "Gwen Stefani") was the first single ever to sell one million digital downloads.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Downloads have been in decline since 2013 as consumers have moved away from a purchase/ownership model towards a [subscription-based model](/wiki/Open_music_model "Open music model") ([streaming](/wiki/Music_streaming "Music streaming")). By 2018, total sales were at their lowest point since the 1970s, and continue to fall. In 2020, major UK retailers such as supermarkets started to stop selling music.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Record_sales&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Marketing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Dusty_Groove_Record_Store_Day_2014.jpg/250px-Dusty_Groove_Record_Store_Day_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dusty_Groove_Record_Store_Day_2014.jpg)

A crowd buying records in the [Dusty Groove](/wiki/Dusty_Groove "Dusty Groove") store during the [Record Store Day](/wiki/Record_Store_Day "Record Store Day"), April 2014.

Aside of paid advertising in print or broadcast media, [radio](/wiki/Radio "Radio") [airplay](/wiki/Airplay "Airplay") is one of the most important tools to sell records. A research commissioned by one of major label groups stated that "four out of five music purchases can be traced to radio airplay.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In 2007, [Record Store Day](/wiki/Record_Store_Day "Record Store Day") was inaugurated to bring together fans, artists, and thousands of independent [record stores](/wiki/Record_store "Record store") across the world.[[28]](#cite_note-usatoday-28) The 2013 event of Record Store Day was credited with the highest U.S. vinyl sales,[[29]](#cite_note-29) and the 2014 edition resulted in independent retailers recording the highest percentage of physical album sales, since the [SoundScan](/wiki/Nielsen_SoundScan "Nielsen SoundScan") system was introduced in 1991.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Merchandise bundles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Record_sales&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Merchandise bundles")]

In 2004, [Prince](/wiki/Prince_(musician) "Prince (musician)") became the first major artist to bundle an album with the purchase of a ticket to a concert. A copy of *[Musicology](/wiki/Musicology_(album) "Musicology (album)")* was given to each audience member who entered the concert venue, and each was counted as an album sale on the week's [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200") chart. This led *Billboard* and Nielsen SoundScan to change their policy on how tickets bundled with albums would count for charting purposes.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

[Travis Scott](/wiki/Travis_Scott "Travis Scott") bundled his 2018 album *[Astroworld](/wiki/Astroworld_(album) "Astroworld (album)")* with his 28-piece merchandise line, which contributed to first-week sales exceeding 270,000 traditional units and a number-one debut on the *Billboard* 200.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

[RIAA](/wiki/RIAA "RIAA") summary by format, in million copies per year.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## Financial loss

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Record_sales&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Financial loss")]

At times, labels overshipped records to retailers with too high expectations of commercial response. Records not sold to consumers may be returned to the label. In January 2008, media reported that over one million unsold copies of [Robbie Williams](/wiki/Robbie_Williams "Robbie Williams")'s album *[Rudebox](/wiki/Rudebox "Rudebox")* were sent to China to be crushed.[[34]](#cite_note-34) [EMI](/wiki/EMI "EMI") owner Guy Hands said, "Roughly 85% of what EMI does get to release never makes a profit, in part because of the cash spent signing bands and partly due to ill-made bets on the number of CDs the market requires for particular acts."[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In 2011, [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)") sold an estimated 440,000 digital copies of [Lady Gaga](/wiki/Lady_Gaga "Lady Gaga")'s *[Born This Way](/wiki/Born_This_Way_(album) "Born This Way (album)")* in its first two days at a price of 99 cents.[[36]](#cite_note-36) This promotional campaign caused a loss of over $3 million for the company.[[37]](#cite_note-37) In 2014, [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2")'s album *[Songs of Innocence](/wiki/Songs_of_Innocence_(U2_album) "Songs of Innocence (U2 album)")* was released for free on [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes "ITunes"). Apple's deal with U2 and the band's label, Universal Music, which stands to lose more than a million full-price sales because of the free download offer, guarantees $100 million worth of high-profile marketing for the album.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## Second-hand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Record_sales&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Second-hand")]

The sale of [second-hand](/wiki/Used_good "Used good") albums and singles continues into the 2020s, with many record stores having a used section, as well as pawn shops such as [CeX](/wiki/CeX_(retailer) "CeX (retailer)"), [Cash Generator](/wiki/Cash_Generator "Cash Generator"), [Cash Converters](/wiki/Cash_Converters "Cash Converters"),[[39]](#cite_note-39) and the emergence of [eBay](/wiki/EBay "EBay"), [MusicMagpie](/wiki/MusicMagpie "MusicMagpie"), [Alibris](/wiki/Alibris "Alibris") and other dedicated websites.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)
